class_schedule.topic: Internship Orientation and Professional Goal Setting
learning_objectives: Understand the academic requirements and deliverables for the internship
Set professional development goals aligned with career aspirations
Establish communication protocols with faculty advisor and site supervisor
readings: NACE Career Competencies for the College Educated
SHRM Code of Ethical and Professional Standards (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: The business internship bridges your academic training and professional career. Your internship is a real job with real expectations. This weekly seminar provides structure for reflecting on your experience and building the professional skills that complement your academic education. This is your chance to test career directions (finance vs marketing vs operations vs consulting vs HR), build a network, and generate material for your resume and portfolio.
key_concepts: Internship requirements: minimum 20 hours per week at an approved business site in a substantive role
Weekly journal expectations: structured reflection connecting work to learning
Communication protocol: bi-weekly check-ins with faculty advisor, weekly with site supervisor
Professional development goal-setting using the SMART framework
Ethical obligations: confidentiality, professional conduct, representing the university
lecture_notes.demonstration: Goal-setting workshop: each student drafts three SMART professional development goals specific to their internship role (e.g., a finance intern might set a goal to build a discounted cash flow model independently by week 8) and receives peer and faculty feedback.
setup_requirements: Digital journal set up and shared with faculty advisor

review.value: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound objectives transform vague aspirations into trackable targets. 'Learn more about finance' becomes 'build three LBO models independently and receive feedback from senior analyst by week 10'

Most business internships involve confidential information: financial data, customer records, strategic plans. NDAs are standard. All course deliverables must be reviewed for compliance before submission. Describe types of problems and methods used without disclosing proprietary details

learning_objectives: Navigate the onboarding process at a professional business organization
Adapt to the organization's norms, tools, and communication styles
Build productive relationships with mentors and colleagues
readings: Robbins and Judge, Organizational Behavior, Ch. 16 (Organizational Culture)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your first weeks at a company set the tone for your entire internship. The ability to onboard quickly, ask effective questions, and adapt to existing processes is more valuable than demonstrating technical brilliance in the first week. Business environments range widely: a quant desk at a hedge fund operates differently than a brand marketing team at a consumer goods company.
key_concepts: Onboarding strategies: reading internal wiki or handbooks before asking questions, shadowing colleagues
Asking effective questions: what you tried, what you expected, what happened
Business meetings: agenda, decorum, preparation, follow-up notes
Email and written communication norms: subject lines, brevity, calls to action
Building credibility through reliability, accuracy, and responsiveness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Round-table discussion: students share first-week experiences, challenges, and strategies that worked. Common patterns: everyone underestimates how much context they need, everyone overestimates how closely senior people are watching them, and everyone benefits from proactive check-ins with their manager.
setup_requirements: Organizational chart or introduction to key colleagues

review.value: Good internships provide small, well-scoped projects that teach the business and tools while contributing real value. If your organization has not assigned one, ask your manager to help identify an appropriate first project rather than waiting

New interns who pepper everyone with their opinions in week one are rarely welcomed. New interns who observe carefully, ask thoughtful questions, and earn context before opining are taken seriously. Observe first, opine later

class_schedule.topic: Business Skills in Practice: Bridging Academy and Industry
learning_objectives: Identify gaps between academic training and professional practice
Develop strategies for rapidly learning tools, systems, and processes on the job
readings: HBR, 'How to Be a Star at Work' (Kelley)
Selected articles on intern effectiveness (provided on LMS)
Examples from Wall Street Prep or Training the Street for finance-focused interns

lecture_notes.introduction: Every intern discovers areas where academic training prepared them well and areas where professional practice differs. Real-world business decisions are made with incomplete information, under time pressure, with political dynamics that textbooks rarely acknowledge. Advanced Excel, financial modeling at scale, CRM systems, project management tools, and internal analytics platforms are often taught on the job.
key_concepts: Common academy-to-industry gaps: messy real data, ambiguous objectives, organizational politics, time pressure
Learning strategies for unfamiliar tools (Excel/PowerBI/Tableau/SQL/Salesforce/Bloomberg)
Professional work standards: accuracy, reliability, meeting deadlines, documentation
Requesting and incorporating feedback productively
Documenting learning for portfolio case studies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Skills gap analysis workshop: each student maps their academic skills against internship role requirements and creates a learning plan. Common gaps identified: Excel beyond basic formulas, presentation design, stakeholder management, working within budgets and deadlines under pressure.
setup_requirements: List of tools and systems used at internship site

review.value: A homework problem has one right answer and is graded. A business problem has many acceptable answers, ambiguous information, and is evaluated by whether it helps someone make a decision. Learning to thrive in ambiguity is the single most valuable internship skill

Despite the rise of Python, R, and BI tools, Excel remains the lingua franca of business. Mastering advanced Excel (pivot tables, lookups, array formulas, keyboard shortcuts, financial functions) pays back daily throughout a business career

learning_objectives: Assess progress toward professional development goals
Articulate the most significant lessons learned so far
Adjust goals and strategies for the second half of the internship
readings: Dweck, Mindset, Ch. 3 (on growth mindset in professional contexts)

lecture_notes.introduction: The midpoint is the time to honestly assess where you are, what you have learned, and what to prioritize in the remaining weeks. Adjusting goals based on experience is a sign of growth, not failure. Often by week 8, interns have discovered that their initial goals were either too ambitious, too modest, or aimed at the wrong target.
key_concepts: Goal progress review: measuring against SMART objectives from week 1
Unexpected learning: skills and insights you did not anticipate
Soliciting supervisor feedback: asking specific questions, not general ones
Networking: professional relationships built and their career value
Adjusting goals for the second half based on actual experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents a 5-minute summary of their internship experience and adjusted goals. Peers and faculty provide feedback. Common themes: 'I thought I would be doing X but actually spend most of my time on Y,' and 'my most valuable learning has been Z, which I did not expect.'

review.value: 'What is one thing I could improve in my financial models?' is more productive than 'How am I doing?' because it invites actionable feedback rather than polite reassurance

Your internship network is one of its most valuable long-term outputs. Schedule 30-minute informational chats with people in adjacent teams. Connect on LinkedIn with a personal note referencing something you discussed. Relationships compound over a career

learning_objectives: Prepare for and participate in a formal supervisor evaluation
Translate internship accomplishments into portfolio-ready documentation
Identify transferable skills developed during the internship

lecture_notes.introduction: Your supervisor evaluation provides external validation of your contributions. Preparing for this evaluation and documenting your work ensures your internship experience translates into career assets: a resume line, an interview story, a LinkedIn update, a portfolio artifact.
key_concepts: Supervisor evaluation preparation: providing context and specific contribution examples
Self-evaluation alignment: comparing self-assessment with supervisor feedback
NDA-compliant portfolio: generalizing proprietary work into demonstrable skills
Transferable skills inventory: analytical thinking, communication, teamwork, leadership
Professional profile updates: resume, LinkedIn, cover letter templates based on new experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio documentation workshop: students practice writing NDA-compliant case studies of their internship work. Example: 'Analyzed customer segmentation data for a leading consumer products company, building a clustering model in Excel that identified three underserved segments representing an estimated $X million opportunity' rather than naming the client or disclosing confidential data.

review.value: Describe the type of problem (not the specific company), the methods and frameworks you applied, and the measurable impact of your work without revealing proprietary details or unannounced initiatives

'Improved reporting turnaround by 60% by automating a weekly workflow in Excel' is stronger than 'worked on reporting improvements.' Whenever possible, attach numbers to your contributions, even rough estimates approved by your supervisor

learning_objectives: Deliver a professional presentation summarizing the internship experience
Articulate a post-graduation career strategy informed by internship experience
Maintain professional relationships after the internship ends
readings: Review all weekly journal entries for synthesis
HBR, 'How to Build a Professional Network' (selected articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your final presentation synthesizes fifteen weeks of professional experience into a narrative. This is practice for interview conversations about your business experience. Employers love the 'tell me about a time when...' question, and your internship should give you material for a dozen such answers.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: context, contributions, challenges, growth, career impact
Storytelling: turning daily work into a compelling professional narrative
Quantifying impact where possible
Lessons learned for your next role
Maintaining professional relationships after the internship
Thank-you notes and LinkedIn connections as career infrastructure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student delivers a 10-minute presentation followed by 5 minutes of Q&A, using the STAR framework (Situation, Task, Action, Result) for key accomplishments. Faculty provides feedback on both the substance and the interview-readiness of the presentation style.
setup_requirements: Thank-you notes drafted for supervisor and colleagues

review.value: Frame your internship as a growth story: what you knew coming in, what challenged you, how you responded to those challenges, and how the experience shaped your professional direction. This structure works in interviews and on cover letters

Professional relationships are career assets. Connect on LinkedIn with a personal note, send occasional check-ins, share articles relevant to their work, and reach out when you have news to share. Networks compound over decades if maintained consistently

major_assignments.title: Internship Plan and Learning Goals
major_assignments.description: Develop a structured internship plan defining professional development goals, communication protocols, and a learning plan for tools and skills required by the role.
objectives: Define three to five SMART professional development goals
Establish communication protocols with supervisor and faculty advisor
Research the organization's products, customers, culture, and business model
Create a learning plan for tools and skills needed
deliverables: Internship plan document (4-5 pages) with SMART goals, communication plan, and learning objectives
Skills gap analysis mapping current abilities against role requirements
Signed internship agreement and supervisor contact information
Initial journal entry documenting expectations and first impressions

criteria: Goals are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound
Goals reflect genuine professional development aspirations
Goals are informed by research into the organization and role
At least one goal addresses a known skill gap
Skills gap analysis is honest and specific
Learning plan includes concrete resources and timelines
Document is well-written and professionally formatted
Journal entry is reflective and substantive
Well-defined SMART goals with genuine professional development intent
Thorough preparation with organization research and skills analysis
Clear communication plan with all parties

major_assignments.title: Mid-Term Reflection
major_assignments.description: Write a comprehensive reflection assessing progress toward goals, documenting key learnings, and adjusting the plan for the remaining weeks.
objectives: Assess progress toward each SMART goal with evidence
Identify the most significant professional lessons learned
Document unexpected challenges and how they were addressed
Revise goals for the remaining weeks based on experience
deliverables: Reflection essay (5-7 pages) with goal progress, learnings, and revised plan
Updated SMART goals with revisions justified

criteria: Honest assessment of progress toward each goal
Evidence supports all claims of growth
Goals remain achievable in remaining time
Connects daily tasks to professional development
Lessons are generalizable beyond this internship
Professional insight connecting experience to career
Effective communication through writing and presentation

major_assignments.title: Supervisor Evaluation and Self-Assessment
major_assignments.description: Coordinate the formal supervisor evaluation, complete a self-evaluation, and analyze the alignment between the two assessments.
objectives: Complete self-evaluation using the same criteria
Analyze alignment and gaps between evaluations
Create action items for final weeks

criteria: Supervisor evaluation complete with substantive comments
Submitted on time demonstrating proactive coordination
Identifies areas of alignment and divergence
Explores divergences with curiosity not defensiveness
Complete evaluation package submitted through proactive coordination

major_assignments.title: Final Report and Presentation
major_assignments.description: Produce a comprehensive final report synthesizing the entire internship experience, including portfolio additions, career impact assessment, and a formal presentation.
objectives: Synthesize the full experience into a professional narrative
Document contributions as NDA-compliant portfolio pieces
Assess the internship's impact on career direction
Deliver a polished presentation demonstrating communication skills
deliverables: Final report (8-12 pages) covering contributions, growth, challenges, and career impact
At least two NDA-compliant portfolio case studies
Thank-you correspondence for supervisor and key colleagues
Updated LinkedIn profile reviewed by peer

criteria: Comprehensive narrative covering the full arc
Challenges discussed honestly with lessons learned
Career impact assessment grounded in experience
At least two substantive case studies
Comprehensive report synthesizing rich internship experience

internship_performance.description: Supervisor evaluation of work quality, professionalism, and contribution

weekly_journals.description: Weekly reflection journals documenting learning, business observations, and growth

final_report.description: Comprehensive internship report with portfolio additions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at the weekly Friday seminar is mandatory. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the grade. Internship site attendance follows the agreed schedule and is monitored by the site supervisor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Weekly journals due every Friday by 5 PM. Late journals penalized 10% per day. Major deadlines are firm. Professional reliability includes meeting deadlines.
course_policies.collaboration: All deliverables are individual work reflecting personal experience. Peer feedback during seminar is expected and welcomed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be your own. Internship work in reports must comply with your NDA. Fabricating experience or accomplishments is a serious violation of academic integrity and professional ethics.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need journal platform access, document editing software, presentation tools, and LinkedIn and resume tools. All deliverables submitted through LMS. Weekly journals must be kept current.
